splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability must be determined by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the period of court-ordered supervision beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects revocation proceedings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Supervised release is tolled during absconding
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled while a supervisee is a fugitive, allowing courts to revoke supervised release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Supervised release is not tolled
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and that the term expires as originally set regardless of the supervisee's fugitive status.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: The circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face Section 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar against raising any claim they previously presented when bringing successive collateral attacks under Section 2255. If it applies, federal prisoners face a stricter gatekeeping requirement than Section 2255(h) alone would impose.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to Section 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive motions under Section 2255, categorically barring previously presented claims.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to Section 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners filing under Section 2255, meaning they need only satisfy Section 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the standard for evaluating whether candidates have pre-election standing to challenge voting rules. The split affects whether candidates can bring pre-election challenges to mail-in voting procedures, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices nationwide.

positions.label: Candidates have standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge state election rules pre-election because competitive injuries from allegedly unlawful vote-counting procedures are sufficiently concrete and imminent.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidates lack standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates do not have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to ballot-counting rules because the alleged injury is too speculative or generalized.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bognet v. Secretary of Commonwealth of Pennsylvania

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination in the operation of places of public accommodation under Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, including the availability of compensatory damages?
splits.description: Circuits have reached different conclusions regarding the standards and remedies available under Title II of the Civil Rights Act for discrimination claims against hospitality and business establishments. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the disagreement, which impacts hospitality, retail, and other service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Broader remedies and standards
positions.summary: Holds that broader remedies, including compensatory damages, may be available for discrimination claims under Title II or related statutes governing places of public accommodation.

positions.label: More restrictive remedies and standards
positions.summary: Holds that Title II provides more limited remedies and applies more restrictive standards for establishing discrimination claims against public accommodations.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps agency or Sentencing Commission deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines that increase criminal penalties.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity should take precedence over deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions in criminal cases. At least three circuits hold that lenity trumps deference, while others (including the Third Circuit panel in Chandler) have held deference prevails over lenity.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over Auer/Kisor deference to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations when construing ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through delegated interpretive power.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that Auer/Kisor deference to the Sentencing Commission's authoritative commentary prevails over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Guidelines provisions, even in criminal sentencing contexts.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (requiring only one criterion to disqualify).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief under the First Step Act only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), or if possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. This affects whether district courts must impose mandatory minimum sentences in drug cases.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive or distributive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as disjunctive or distributive in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) encompasses inchoate offenses like conspiracy, based on Application Note 1's expansion of the guideline text.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds inchoate offenses (conspiracy, attempt, aiding and abetting) to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation entitled to Stinson/Kisor deference. Some circuits defer to the Note while others hold the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is valid — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses in the definition of 'controlled substance offense' is entitled to deference as an authoritative interpretation of an ambiguous guideline provision under Stinson and Kisor.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is invalid — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously does not include inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline beyond its plain meaning, particularly after Kisor's narrowing of deference principles.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically prohibit it, while others permit it either alone or in combination with other factors. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) allowing it has further complicated the split.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may be considered, alone or in combination with other factors, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot contribute
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, whether offered alone or in combination with other factors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state drug schedule is broader than the federal Controlled Substances Act and covers substances not federally controlled.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the underlying substance to be federally controlled, or whether any state-controlled substance suffices. This affects career offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State schedule controls — no federal limitation required
positions.summary: Holds that 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines includes offenses under state law regardless of whether the particular substance is also controlled under federal law, because the text refers broadly to state law offenses.

positions.label: Federal schedule controls — substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a prior state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the initial Chapter 13 filing and the subsequent conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, while other circuits have held it belongs to the debtor.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that under the broad scope of § 541(a) and § 348(f)(1)(A), post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) was designed to protect good-faith converting debtors, and post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the debtor, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appellate filing deadline applies to an appeal from the denial of a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees, though filed in a criminal case, is a civil matter subject to the 30-day civil notice-of-appeal deadline or a criminal matter subject to the 14-day criminal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and thus the 30-day civil notice-of-appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that because the Hyde Amendment motion arises in a criminal case, the shorter 14-day criminal notice-of-appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may expressly impose a sentence upon revocation of supervised release as punishment for the violation of supervised release conditions, or only to sanction the breach of the court's trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the proper purpose of imprisonment upon revocation of supervised release. Some circuits allow courts to punish defendants for the underlying violation conduct, while others hold that revocation sentences must be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust inherent in violating supervisory conditions.

positions.label: Punishment for violation is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release conditions when imposing revocation sentences, not merely sanction the breach of trust.

positions.label: Only breach-of-trust sanctioning is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that sentences upon revocation of supervised release should be based on the breach of trust, not punishment for the new criminal conduct, consistent with the Sentencing Commission's policy statements.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must bring challenges to the conditions or execution of their confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement) exclusively through habeas corpus, or may also use Bivens or other civil rights causes of action.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy for federal prisoner challenges that affect conditions of confinement but also implicate the duration or execution of a sentence. The Eighth Circuit in Fortner v. Eischen highlighted this unresolved conflict regarding First Step Act earned time credits and prerelease custody decisions.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for duration-affecting claims
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner's challenge to conditions of confinement effectively seeks earlier release or challenges the execution of a sentence, habeas corpus is the exclusive federal remedy.

positions.label: Civil rights actions also available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may pursue civil rights claims (under § 1983 or Bivens) alongside or instead of habeas even when the challenge relates to the execution of a sentence, so long as they are not directly challenging the fact or duration of confinement.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Carmona v. United States Bureau of Prisons

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether the FAAAA's preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier... with respect to the transportation of property,' extends to state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for injuries caused by carriers they selected.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that the FAAAA preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers because such claims are 'related to' the broker's services in arranging transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they are too tenuously connected to the broker's transportation services to fall within the FAAAA's preemption clause.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label that the EPA did not require. The question affects thousands of product liability cases involving agricultural chemicals.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims when they are based on duties that parallel rather than add to FIFRA's requirements, consistent with Bates v. Dow Agrosciences.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas-type challenges — such as arguments about errors in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits permit courts to consider such claims while others hold compassionate release cannot serve as a substitute for habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider factors related to potential errors in a defendant's conviction or sentence, including claims that could also be raised in habeas, as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis.

positions.label: May not consider habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release is not a substitute for habeas corpus and courts may not rely on challenges to the validity of a conviction or sentence as extraordinary and compelling reasons for release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical records are immediately appealable as collateral orders because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' effectively unreviewable after final judgment. This creates tension with other circuits that have more restrictively applied the collateral order doctrine to sealing disputes.

positions.label: Immediately appealable under collateral order doctrine
positions.summary: Holds that denial of a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable because maintaining privacy in medical treatments and diagnoses is a value of high order that cannot be adequately vindicated by post-judgment appeal.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal, even those involving sensitive information, do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's requirements because the privacy interest can be adequately protected through other means or addressed on appeal from final judgment.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: In re City of New York

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to define the class of 'transportation workers' exempt from the FAA under Section 1, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who transport goods across state lines or distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce qualify for the exemption.

positions.label: Broad reading — delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods that have moved in interstate commerce, including last-mile distributors of baked goods, are transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow reading — delivery workers do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that last-mile delivery workers are not 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the FAA because they do not work in the transportation industry.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
